By mapping this to the available atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' indicates all self-test steps passed successfully, and 'testAlarmsPass' indicates a successful test of the alarms. Thus, the structured NL expresses that whenever the self-test passes, the alarm test also must pass—capturing the notion that the self-test must ensure both overall system and alarm functionality.

explanation: The input requirement is about ensuring that the system has a self-test procedure to verify that the system and its accessories are fully functional and that alarms work. The atomic proposition 'selfTestPass' encapsulates the result of all self-test procedures passing successfully. To express that the self-test ensures both the system/accessories are functional and that alarms work, we rely on the outcome signaled by 'selfTestPass'. It is reasonable to formalize this as a property that must always hold—i.e., whenever the self-test procedure occurs, it must result in a pass, thus ensuring all covered functionality and alarms are operational whenever a self-test is completed. Since there is no atom directly representing the running of the self-test, but only the result ('selfTestPass'), we use 'always satisfy selfTestPass' to encode the intent that successful self-test is a required invariant.

The available atomic propositions point to 'selfTestPass' (indicates all self-test steps passed successfully) and 'testAlarmsPass' (indicates local alarms test pass (success)) as the direct indicators of this. Since the requirement does not specify a temporal condition, the property can be phrased as: 'eventually satisfy (selfTestPass & testAlarmsPass)'. There are no discrete propositions for 'accessories', but since the self-test is assumed to check the system as a whole, 'selfTestPass & testAlarmsPass' as a boolean expression captures that both the system (including accessories, as part of selfTest) and alarms are confirmed functional.
